topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a dropped stone in a still pond. Dr. Albright was leaving. My world, meticulously structured around the contours of his expertise, suddenly felt like it was tilting off its axis. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the library buzzing with an irritating, high-pitched whine. I didn't feel sadness, not exactly. Instead, a tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, a physical resistance to accepting the sudden void. I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic cool against my fingertips, and began aggressively underlining passages in my draft. *No. This isn't finished.* I slammed the notebook shut, the sound sharp in the quiet study room. I needed more time, more clarity, and I wasn't going to let this departure derail the progress I’d made. I started drafting an email, the words forming slowly, deliberately, each sentence a small act of defiance against the impending change.

[story 2]

"So, he’s actually going?" I asked, the words tasting like dust in my mouth. The question hung in the air between me and Sarah, who was nervously tapping her foot by the water cooler. I felt a strange hardening in my jaw. It wasn't fear, exactly; it was a stubborn refusal to acknowledge the reality of the situation. If Dr. Albright was leaving, then the established roadmap for my research—the very scaffolding I’d built over the last two years—was suddenly unstable. I stood up, pacing the narrow aisle between the stacks, my footsteps echoing too loudly. I needed to re-read every footnote, to re-verify every citation. My fingers traced the spine of a heavy textbook, a repetitive, almost frantic motion. *I will figure this out,* I thought, a fierce, internal resolve taking root. I wouldn't let a change in personnel dictate the pace of my work.

[story 3]

The announcement had been delivered in a hushed departmental meeting, and I had managed a tight, neutral expression. Internally, however, a slow, simmering resentment began to bubble up. Dr. Albright, with his meticulous critiques and his unwavering belief in my potential, was simply abandoning ship. It felt like a betrayal of the entire process. I retreated to my desk, pulling my chair in close, creating a physical barrier between myself and the news. I picked up my laptop, the screen glowing with the daunting expanse of my remaining chapters. Instead of panicking, I started typing a lengthy rebuttal to my own initial outline, rewriting sections with increased intensity, pushing the language harder than before. Every keystroke was a declaration: *I control this narrative. I control the outcome.* The discomfort in my shoulders was a testament to the sheer willpower I was exerting to keep moving forward, ignoring the unsettling shift in the academic landscape.
